content: Hey Saturday — can you help me plan our migration from Create React App to Vite? I’m leading this with James Park and need something concrete we can take to the team.

Absolutely — great initiative, and this is exactly the kind of migration that pays dividends quickly if you structure it well. Since you’re partnering with James Park as frontend lead, I’d frame this as a delivery plan with measurable performance wins, low-risk rollout checkpoints, and explicit fallback paths. I’ll give you a practical blueprint you can turn into an RFC and execution tracker.

First, anchor the why in objective numbers and developer pain. Your benchmark is already compelling: CRA build/dev startup around 94 seconds versus Vite at 3.1 seconds in equivalent local conditions. That’s not just a “nice-to-have.” Across a team, that reduction compounds into materially faster feedback loops, fewer context switches, and better PR throughput. Make sure you capture exactly what was measured: cold start time, HMR latency, production build time, and test execution startup overhead. Include machine specs used for benchmarking (e.g., M2 Pro / 32GB / Node 20.11), because engineering teams will ask.

I’d structure migration into five workstreams:
1) Build tooling and config parity.
2) Test runner migration (Jest → Vitest).
3) Environment variable and runtime config changes.
4) CSS and asset pipeline behavior alignment.
5) Phased rollout with rollback strategy.

For build tooling parity, define “must-match behavior” before touching implementation: path aliases, SVG handling, static assets, code splitting, chunk naming constraints, source maps, proxy behavior, and TypeScript transpile/check strategy. CRA hides many defaults via react-scripts; Vite makes these explicit, so your team needs a checklist to avoid silent behavior drift.

A minimal target Vite setup usually includes @vitejs/plugin-react, resolve.alias mirroring tsconfig paths, define flags for build-time constants, server.proxy for API parity, and build.rollupOptions only where needed. Keep config small initially — avoid overfitting with plugins until baseline equivalence is proven.

For migration governance, I strongly recommend James owning a weekly “parity matrix” review: each row is a behavior category, each column includes CRA current behavior, Vite target behavior, status, risk, owner, and validation method. This avoids vague progress reports and helps QA and platform engineers participate meaningfully.

Execution cadence suggestion:
- Week 1: Baseline benchmarks + inventory + parity matrix + RFC draft.
- Week 2: Stand up Vite branch with app booting and route shell rendering.
- Week 3: Asset/CSS parity + env var conversion + dev proxy verification.
- Week 4: Vitest dual-run setup + CI lane prototype.
- Week 5: Pilot release to internal users behind deployment ring.
- Week 6: 10% traffic canary (if web app architecture supports) or selected tenant rollout.
- Week 7: 50% rollout + remove major blockers.
- Week 8: Full cutover, CRA freeze, rollback window, then decommission.

Define success criteria now, not later:
- Local dev startup <= 5s median.
- HMR update <= 300ms for common component edits.
- Production bundle size delta within agreed threshold (e.g., +/- 5% initially).
- Test runtime no worse than Jest baseline in CI by GA date.
- Zero P0/P1 regressions in first two weeks post-cutover.

Also set explicit non-goals to keep scope controlled. Example: “No architecture refactor during tool migration” and “No design system rewrite in this phase.” Tool migrations become risky when teams try to modernize everything simultaneously.

If you want, next I can draft:
- a side-by-side CRA vs Vite config mapping,
- a concrete Jest-to-Vitest config conversion,
- env var and CSS Modules gotchas checklist,
- and a milestone/rollback runbook James can present in planning.
Yes, let’s do the side-by-side config mapping first. We currently have craco for aliases and some webpack tweaks. Need specifics.

Perfect — if you’re on CRA + CRACO, the migration is usually smoother than raw CRA because you’ve already made implicit behavior explicit. Below is a practical side-by-side mapping you and James can convert directly into implementation tasks.

Start by inventorying your current CRACO surface area:
- webpack.alias entries
- webpack.plugins additions (DefinePlugin, IgnorePlugin, BundleAnalyzerPlugin, etc.)
- Babel customizations
- devServer proxy settings
- style loaders overrides (CSS Modules naming, Sass include paths)
- fallback polyfills (crypto, stream, buffer)

Then map each to Vite equivalents:

1) Entry, HTML template, and environment injection
CRA: public/index.html with %REACT_APP_*% interpolation and script injection via react-scripts.
Vite: root index.html treated as part of module graph; env interpolation via import.meta.env and %VITE_*% in HTML where relevant.
Action: Move template placeholders, remove CRA-specific tokens, validate favicon/manifest paths.

2) Aliases
CRACO webpack.alias:
- '@components': 'src/components'
- '@lib': 'src/lib'
Vite equivalent:
- resolve.alias with absolute path resolution using fileURLToPath/new URL or path.resolve.
Also align tsconfig compilerOptions.paths to match exactly. If aliases diverge between TS and bundler, developers get compile-vs-runtime drift.

3) Webpack DefinePlugin
If you inject constants like __APP_VERSION__ or process.env.BUILD_SHA:
Vite define supports literal replacement:
- define: { __APP_VERSION__: JSON.stringify(pkg.version), __BUILD_SHA__: JSON.stringify(process.env.GIT_SHA || 'dev') }
Important: do not rely on process.env at runtime in browser code; Vite favors import.meta.env for exposed vars.

4) Dev server proxy
CRACO devServer.proxy to /api backend.
Vite server.proxy provides comparable behavior, including path rewrites and ws support.
Example parity checks:
- Cookie forwarding behavior
- Secure false for self-signed certs in staging
- WebSocket path upgrades
- Timeout behavior for long-running endpoints

5) Babel and transpilation
CRA relies heavily on Babel pipeline.
Vite (React plugin) uses esbuild for transform and can use Babel where plugin-react applies Fast Refresh transforms.
If you depend on bespoke Babel plugins (e.g., macros, styled-components plugin options), explicitly configure via plugin-react babel option or alternative plugin support. Validate before assuming parity.

6) SVG handling
CRA supports importing SVG as ReactComponent via SVGR convention.
In Vite, you typically add vite-plugin-svgr and adjust imports or keep compatibility style.
Task: grep for “ReactComponent as” imports and run codemod if needed.

7) Static assets and public folder
CRA public folder copied as-is; references often expect process.env.PUBLIC_URL.
Vite uses /public similarly but base path handling is via base config and import semantics.
Action: replace PUBLIC_URL-dependent concatenation with new URL/import patterns where appropriate.

8) Node polyfills
CRA/webpack historically masked some Node core module usage.
Vite is stricter. If frontend code imports crypto/stream/path unintentionally, fix code first; only polyfill intentionally. Keep a temporary plugin/polyfill layer if immediate cleanup is too big, but track as debt.

9) CSS/Sass pipeline
CRACO may alter css-loader modules config.
Vite css.modules supports localsConvention, generateScopedName, and preprocessorOptions for Sass includePaths/additionalData.
Ensure class name hashing and camelCase behavior match old expectations to avoid runtime undefined class lookups.

10) Source maps and build output
CRA source maps default patterns may differ from Vite.
Set build.sourcemap policy (true/hidden) by environment and document Sentry upload impacts. Validate chunk file naming if your CDN cache policies depend on specific patterns.

Suggested starter vite.config.ts shape (conceptual):
- plugins: [react(), svgr()]
- resolve.alias: mirrored from CRACO
- server.proxy: API/WebSocket parity
- define: build constants
- css.modules + preprocessorOptions
- build.rollupOptions only where needed

Migration workflow James can run:
A) Create “config parity checklist” with ~30 checks.
B) Build minimal Vite config and boot app shell.
C) Reintroduce customizations one at a time, validating with smoke tests after each.
D) Lock parity with snapshot docs before optimization.

Key pitfall to avoid: porting every webpack-era tweak blindly. Many are obsolete in Vite and can introduce complexity. Tag each tweak as Required / Nice-to-have / Legacy and only migrate Required for phase 1.

If you want, next I’ll give a concrete Jest→Vitest migration template (including setupFiles, jsdom, coverage, moduleNameMapper-to-alias mapping, and CI commands) you can paste into your repo.
Great. Let’s do Jest to Vitest now. We have setupTests.ts, jest-dom, some API mocks, and about 1,900 tests.

Excellent — with 1,900 tests, you’ll want a controlled dual-run period rather than a hard cutover. The best pattern is “Vitest shadow mode” in CI for 1–2 weeks: Jest remains gating while Vitest runs in parallel and reports parity delta. Once pass rate and runtime stabilize, flip the gate.

Here’s a concrete migration model.

1) Install and baseline config
Add dev deps:
- vitest
- @vitest/coverage-v8 (or c8/istanbul variant if needed)
- jsdom
- @testing-library/jest-dom
- @testing-library/react
- @testing-library/user-event
- msw (if you mock APIs with service worker pattern)

In vite.config.ts add test block (or vitest.config.ts if you prefer separation):
- test.environment = 'jsdom'
- test.globals = true (optional; if false, import describe/it/expect explicitly)
- test.setupFiles = ['./src/setupTests.ts']
- test.css = true (if components import CSS)
- test.coverage.provider = 'v8'
- test.coverage.reporter = ['text', 'lcov', 'html']
- test.include/exclude patterns matching Jest
- test.restoreMocks/mockReset/clearMocks as needed

2) setupTests.ts conversion
Most jest-dom setup remains identical:
- import '@testing-library/jest-dom';
If you used Jest globals in setup (e.g., jest.spyOn, jest.useFakeTimers), swap to vi.* equivalents.
For fetch/polyfills, ensure they’re loaded in setup.
If you used custom matchers with expect.extend, still supported.

3) API mocks
If you currently use manual jest.mock on API modules, Vitest supports vi.mock with mostly similar semantics, but hoisting behavior can differ in edge cases.
If you use MSW:
- Start server beforeAll
- resetHandlers afterEach
- close afterAll
This pattern ports cleanly.
If using axios mocks or fetch mocks, ensure reset logic uses vi.restoreAllMocks/vi.clearAllMocks in afterEach to prevent cross-test contamination.

4) Common Jest -> Vitest API mapping
- jest.fn() -> vi.fn()
- jest.spyOn() -> vi.spyOn()
- jest.mock() -> vi.mock()
- jest.requireActual() -> vi.importActual()
- jest.useFakeTimers() -> vi.useFakeTimers()
- jest.advanceTimersByTime() -> vi.advanceTimersByTime()
- jest.setSystemTime() -> vi.setSystemTime()
- jest.resetModules() -> vi.resetModules()

Not all behaviors are 100% identical; timer and module mocking edge cases should be tracked separately.

5) moduleNameMapper and aliases
Jest moduleNameMapper often maps:
- '^@/(.*)$' -> '<rootDir>/src/$1'
- '\\.(css|less|scss)$' -> identity-obj-proxy
In Vitest, alias resolution should come from Vite resolve.alias.
For CSS modules in tests, Vitest generally handles through Vite pipeline; avoid over-mocking unless necessary. If snapshot outputs differ, standardize serializer strategy.

6) ESM/CJS differences
Vitest is ESM-native leaning. If your tests rely on CommonJS-only patterns, you may need:
- small refactors in imports
- dynamic import for conditionally loaded modules
- package.json type/module interoperability checks
This is one of the top causes of early failures, so categorize and batch-fix.

7) Coverage parity strategy
Jest coverage often uses Istanbul instrumentation; Vitest with v8 may produce slightly different branch counts.
Do not block cutover on exact percentage equality. Instead set tolerance bands per package (e.g., +/-1.5%).
For governance, James can publish a weekly “coverage delta report” explaining any structural metric shifts.

8) CI rollout plan for tests
Phase A (Week 1):
- Add vitest command non-blocking: npm run test:vitest -- --run
- Collect pass rate and runtime
Phase B (Week 2):
- Fail PR if new tests only pass in Jest but fail in Vitest for changed files
Phase C (Week 3):
- Switch required gate to Vitest, keep Jest informational
Phase D (Week 4):
- Remove Jest config/deps, archive migration notes

Suggested scripts:
- "test": "jest"
- "test:vitest": "vitest"
- "test:vitest:ci": "vitest run --coverage"
- later flip "test" to vitest

9) Performance expectations
For 1,900 tests, Vitest typically improves startup and watch responsiveness significantly. CI full-run gains vary by test design and I/O. Track:
- cold run total duration
- changed-file watch rerun latency
- flaky test count

10) Risk register items
- Snapshot diff churn due to serializer/runtime differences
- Fake timer behavior differences
- Implicit Jest globals in legacy tests
- Module mock hoisting assumptions

I recommend creating an “incompatibility bucket” label and triaging daily during migration week.

If you want, I can next draft exact environment variable migration rules (REACT_APP_ → VITE_, import.meta.env usage, .env.* precedence, and safe runtime config pattern for prod deployments).
Yes please. Env vars are messy for us. We have REACT_APP_API_URL, REACT_APP_SENTRY_DSN, and some accidental secrets in .env.local.

Great callout — env handling is one of the highest-risk areas in CRA → Vite migrations because it blends security, deployment behavior, and developer ergonomics. The short version: in Vite, only variables prefixed with VITE_ are exposed to client bundles by default, and access shifts from process.env.REACT_APP_* to import.meta.env.VITE_*. That’s safer than many CRA setups, but only if you clean house properly.

Here’s a concrete migration framework James can run.

1) Classify every variable before renaming
Create an env inventory with columns:
- Name
- Current source (.env, CI secret, runtime injection)
- Used in client or server
- Sensitivity (public/internal/secret)
- Target name
- Owner

For your examples:
- REACT_APP_API_URL -> likely public client config -> VITE_API_URL
- REACT_APP_SENTRY_DSN -> usually public-ish DSN (not auth secret) -> VITE_SENTRY_DSN
- Accidental secrets in .env.local -> remove immediately, rotate if exposed

Important principle: if it’s in client code, treat it as public. Prefix is not encryption; it’s exposure control.

2) Rename and refactor usage
Code migration pattern:
- process.env.REACT_APP_API_URL -> import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
- process.env.NODE_ENV checks -> import.meta.env.MODE or import.meta.env.DEV/PROD
Also remember Vite env values are strings by default. Explicitly parse booleans/numbers:
- const featureX = import.meta.env.VITE_FEATURE_X === 'true'
- const timeoutMs = Number(import.meta.env.VITE_TIMEOUT_MS ?? 5000)

3) Define env file strategy and precedence
Recommend standardized files:
- .env (shared defaults, non-sensitive)
- .env.development
- .env.test
- .env.production
- .env.local (ignored; developer-local overrides, never committed)
- .env.production.local (usually avoid in CI-managed setups)

Document precedence clearly for team onboarding. Also ensure .gitignore includes local env overrides and secrets files.

4) Eliminate accidental secret leakage
Given you already found accidental secrets in .env.local, do three steps now:
A) Audit repo history for leaked keys/tokens.
B) Rotate anything that might have been committed or shared in screenshots/logs.
C) Add secret scanning in CI/pre-commit (e.g., gitleaks/trufflehog style tooling).

Even if .env.local is gitignored, secrets can leak via copied snippets, CI debug echo, or error logs.

5) Add typed env guardrail
Create a small env module (e.g., src/config/env.ts) that validates required vars at startup using zod/envalid/custom checks.
Example policy:
- Required in all modes: VITE_API_URL
- Required in production: VITE_SENTRY_DSN
- Optional flags with defaults: VITE_ENABLE_X = false

This prevents silent undefined behavior and catches misconfigured deployments early.

6) Runtime vs build-time config decision
Vite env is build-time by default. If you need “build once, deploy many environments,” consider runtime-injected config (e.g., window.__APP_CONFIG__ loaded from config.js or JSON endpoint).
Decision rule:
- Stable per-build values -> VITE_* build-time
- Environment-specific post-build values -> runtime config
If your current release pipeline rebuilds per env, build-time may be fine. If not, adopt runtime config pattern now to avoid future pain.

7) Sentry-specific handling
DSN is usually okay in client bundle, but auth tokens (for source map upload) must stay in CI only.
So:
- VITE_SENTRY_DSN in client env files
- SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN only in CI secret store, never VITE_-prefixed
Double-check source map upload scripts don’t log token-bearing commands.

8) CI/CD updates
Update pipeline variables from REACT_APP_* to VITE_*.
Temporarily support both for transition (1 sprint max), with fallback logic in env.ts and deprecation warning logs.
After cutoff date, remove fallback to avoid permanent dual naming debt.

9) Verification checklist before cutover
- grep for process.env.REACT_APP_ returns zero results
- grep for process.env usage in client code minimized/justified
- startup validation fails fast when required VITE_* missing
- production build contains expected public vars only
- no secrets detected in built assets (spot-check with string scan)

10) Suggested milestone dates (example)
- May 6: Env inventory complete, secret audit started
- May 8: Rename PR merged (dual-read compatibility)
- May 10: CI variables switched to VITE_*
- May 13: Remove dual-read fallback
- May 15: Secret rotation complete and documented

If you want, next I can cover CSS Modules behavior differences in detail (naming conventions, camelCase exports, composition, injection order, and how to avoid subtle regressions).
Do that. CSS Modules are gonna be painful; we have mixed .module.scss and global styles, plus some :global hacks.

Totally understandable — CSS is where teams feel “death by a thousand papercuts” during bundler migration. The good news is you can de-risk this with a strict parity checklist and a few explicit Vite settings. Since you have mixed .module.scss, globals, and :global hacks, you need to lock behavior intentionally instead of trusting defaults.

Core differences to account for:

1) Module detection and naming
CRA (via css-loader) and Vite both support *.module.scss, but generated class hash patterns may differ. If your tests/snapshots or runtime logic depend on stable class strings (they shouldn’t, but many apps do indirectly), define generateScopedName in Vite css.modules to approximate previous pattern.

Action:
- Set css.modules.generateScopedName with a deterministic format.
- Confirm no code parses class names for analytics/test selectors.

2) Export conventions (camelCase vs dashes)
If you have class names like .user-card and access styles.userCard, behavior depends on localsConvention.
In Vite:
- css.modules.localsConvention can be 'camelCaseOnly', 'camelCase', etc.
Pick the mode that matches current usage. Run static analysis for bracket access patterns (styles['user-card']) and dot-access styles.userCard to avoid undefined class mappings.

3) :global usage semantics
Your “:global hacks” are common in legacy module systems. Validate these patterns:
- :global(.foo) inside module files
- nested :global with Sass nesting
- global resets imported from module boundaries
Small parser differences can alter selector output.

Recommendation:
- Catalog all :global occurrences (grep).
- Classify as intentional override vs legacy workaround.
- For intentional cases, add visual regression coverage around affected components.
- For legacy hacks, create cleanup tickets post-migration rather than rewriting during cutover.

4) Injection order and cascade behavior
Webpack and Vite can differ in CSS injection order during dev, especially with code-split modules and dynamic imports. This can surface as “works on refresh, breaks on navigation.”

Mitigation:
- Identify critical cascade dependencies (theme, typography, utility layers).
- Move foundational global CSS imports to one predictable root entry.
- Avoid relying on incidental import order deep in component tree.
- If needed, adopt CSS cascade layers for clearer precedence model.

5) Sass include paths and shared variables
If CRA/CRACO had Sass loader includePaths or automatic imports, replicate via Vite css.preprocessorOptions.scss.
Typical needs:
- includePaths for shared styles directory
- additionalData for global mixins/tokens injection
Be cautious: global automatic injection can mask missing explicit dependencies and produce larger compiled CSS; preserve current behavior first, optimize later.

6) URL handling in CSS
Asset URL rewriting can differ subtly:
- background: url('../assets/x.svg')
Validate compiled paths for nested scss partials and public asset references. Prefer explicit asset imports from JS where practical for stronger build guarantees.

7) CSS Modules + testing behavior
Jest often used identity-obj-proxy, which returns readable keys and can hide real integration issues. Vitest with Vite pipeline may behave closer to actual runtime.
Expect some snapshot churn.

Plan:
- For brittle snapshots, pivot assertions from raw class strings to semantic DOM queries.
- Keep a temporary serializer strategy if needed while stabilizing.

8) Global stylesheet boundaries
Since you have both modules and globals, define and document boundaries:
- globals: reset, typography, design tokens, utility classes
- modules: component-scoped styles
- prohibited: component module reaching into unrelated global containers unless explicitly justified
This governance reduces future regressions and helps onboarding.

9) Regression detection strategy
Use three layers:
A) Static checks: grep for risky patterns (:global, !important proliferation, deep nesting).
B) Unit/integration: component tests for class toggling logic.
C) Visual regression: Storybook/Chromatic/Percy-style checks on top 80 components and key layouts.

For migration, visual checks are your best ROI for CSS parity.

10) Practical phased hardening
Week 1: Establish Vite css.modules and Sass preprocessor parity.
Week 2: Fix obvious undefined class export issues.
Week 3: Run visual regression on critical paths (auth, dashboard, checkout/settings equivalents).
Week 4: Triage :global anomalies; defer non-critical cleanup.

Suggested Vite CSS config knobs to decide explicitly:
- css.modules.localsConvention
- css.modules.generateScopedName
- css.preprocessorOptions.scss.includePaths
- css.preprocessorOptions.scss.additionalData
- devSourcemap if debugging style origin matters

Finally, for James’s team presentation, include a “known acceptable diffs” list (e.g., non-user-visible class hash changes) versus “release blockers” (layout shift, theming regressions, broken responsive breakpoints). This prevents subjective debates during QA.

If you want, I can now draft the end-to-end phased rollout plan with concrete milestones, owners, success metrics, and a rollback runbook (including trigger thresholds and revert steps).
Yes — give me the phased rollout + rollback plan. Include dates and who owns what. We want to present next Tuesday, June 11.

Absolutely — here’s a concrete rollout and rollback plan you can present on Tuesday, June 11. I’ll assume a start immediately after approval and target a controlled production cutover over ~6 weeks. I’ll include owners, milestones, decision gates, and explicit rollback triggers.

Program framing
- Program name: “Frontend Build Modernization: CRA → Vite”
- Executive sponsor: Eng Manager (you can fill name)
- Frontend lead: James Park
- Technical PM/coordinator: You
- Supporting owners:
  - Tooling/Build: Priya N.
  - Test migration: Mateo R.
  - Env/Security: Alina K.
  - CSS/UX parity: Dana L.
  - CI/CD + Release: Victor H.

Baseline metrics to publish on June 11
- CRA local startup: 94s median cold start
- Vite local startup: 3.1s median cold start
- Current Jest suite: 1,900 tests, baseline CI runtime recorded
- Current bundle size + source map upload timings recorded

Timeline and milestones

Milestone 0 — Approval & Freeze Scope (Tue Jun 11 – Wed Jun 12)
Owner: James Park + You
Deliverables:
- Approved RFC with non-goals (“no architecture rewrite,” “no design system refactor”)
- Parity matrix template finalized
- Risk register opened
Gate:
- Team sign-off on scope boundaries and success criteria

Milestone 1 — Tooling Parity Branch (Thu Jun 13 – Wed Jun 19)
Owner: Priya N.
Deliverables:
- Vite app boots, core routes render
- Alias/proxy/SVG/assets parity implemented
- Initial env var dual-read compatibility in place
- Build benchmark script committed
Success criteria:
- Dev startup <= 5s on reference machine
- No blocker-level routing or asset regressions in smoke tests

Milestone 2 — Test Shadow Mode (Thu Jun 20 – Wed Jun 26)
Owner: Mateo R.
Deliverables:
- Vitest configured with jsdom, setupTests.ts, jest-dom, coverage
- CI runs Jest (gating) + Vitest (non-gating)
- Incompatibility triage board active
Success criteria:
- Vitest pass rate >= 97% by end of week
- Top failure classes categorized with owners

Milestone 3 — Env/Security + CSS Parity Hardening (Thu Jun 27 – Wed Jul 3)
Owners: Alina K. (env/security), Dana L. (CSS)
Deliverables:
- REACT_APP_* to VITE_* migration complete
- Secret audit + rotation completed for exposed values
- CSS Modules conventions locked (localsConvention, scoped naming)
- Visual regression pass on critical journeys
Success criteria:
- Zero required client vars unresolved in staging
- No P1 visual regressions on defined critical screens

Milestone 4 — Staging Cutover + Internal Dogfood (Fri Jul 5 – Wed Jul 10)
Owners: Victor H. + James Park
Deliverables:
- Staging default build switched to Vite
- Internal users and QA on Vite build
- Observability dashboard (error rate, web vitals, JS load failures)
Success criteria:
- Error rate within +/-10% of CRA staging baseline
- No new Sev-1 issues for 3 consecutive days

Milestone 5 — Production Canary (Thu Jul 11 – Wed Jul 17)
Owners: Victor H. (release), You (coordination)
Rollout:
- 10% traffic/tenant canary for 48h
- 25% for next 48h
- 50% by end of window if stable
Success criteria:
- Frontend fatal error rate does not exceed baseline by >0.2%
- Core funnel conversion not degraded >1.5%
- Support ticket anomaly threshold not breached

Milestone 6 — Full Cutover + Decommission Window (Thu Jul 18 – Fri Jul 26)
Owners: James Park + Priya N.
Deliverables:
- 100% on Vite
- CRA pipeline frozen (no feature merges to legacy build)
- Jest removed after one week of stable Vitest gating
- Post-migration retrospective
Success criteria:
- 7 days stable production
- All rollback artifacts archived, decommission complete

Rollback strategy (must be rehearsed)

Rollback levels
Level 1: Canary halt
- Trigger: error spike above threshold at 10–25% rollout
- Action: stop percentage increase, hold traffic, investigate

Level 2: Partial revert to CRA build
- Trigger: sustained Sev-2+ user impact >60 min or funnel drop >1.5%
- Action: route all new sessions to CRA artifact; keep Vite for internal debugging only

Level 3: Full rollback
- Trigger: Sev-1 incident, auth/navigation breakage, or widespread JS bootstrap failure
- Action: immediate traffic shift to last known good CRA release

Operational rollback prerequisites
- CRA build pipeline preserved until Jul 26
- Last 3 CRA artifacts retained and deployable
- Feature flag or routing control to shift traffic without code merge
- Runbook with pager roles and comms template in Slack/email/status page

Rollback runbook sequence (target <15 min)
1) Incident commander declares rollback level
2) Release engineer executes traffic switch/artifact redeploy
3) QA validates smoke suite (login, dashboard, key transaction)
4) Observability confirms error normalization
5) Comms posted to stakeholders within 10 minutes

Go/No-Go governance
- Twice-weekly migration standup (Mon/Thu)
- Formal Go/No-Go meetings at:
  - Jul 3 (before staging default)
  - Jul 10 (before prod canary)
  - Jul 17 (before 100% rollout)
Attendees: James, owners, QA lead, SRE/on-call rep, product representative.

Presentation-ready summary for June 11 slide
- Proven performance gain: 94s -> 3.1s startup
- Risk-managed phased migration over 6 weeks
- Dual-run test and env hardening before production
- Explicit thresholds and <15 min rollback path
- Clear ownership and decision gates

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page RACI + checklist format and a concise speaker script James can use in the Tuesday presentation.
